intro: If you have a CSV full of raw rows and want to see totals, averages, or counts grouped by category, you need a pivot chart. Making a pivot chart from a CSV typically means importing the file into Excel or Google Sheets, building a pivot table first, then charting from it. That works, but it takes time. Distill skips all of that: drop in your CSV and it auto-groups and charts your data in seconds.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Pivot Chart?
whatIs.body: A pivot chart is a chart built from a pivot table. A pivot table summarizes raw rows by grouping them on one or more dimensions, then aggregating a numeric column (sum, count, average, etc.). The resulting chart shows those grouped summaries visually. It is most useful when your CSV has many rows that belong to a small number of categories.

whenToUse.heading: When Should You Use a Pivot Chart?
bullets: You have a transaction log or event log and want to see totals by category, region, or time period.
You need to compare a metric (like revenue or count) across multiple groups without writing formulas.
Your CSV has repeated category values and a raw bar chart would show one bar per row instead of one per group.

excelSteps.heading: How to Make a Pivot Chart in Excel from a CSV
steps: Open Excel. Go to File > Open and select your CSV file.
Click any cell inside your data, then go to Insert > PivotTable. In the dialog, choose 'Existing Worksheet' and pick a blank area, then click OK.
In the PivotTable Fields panel, drag the column you want to group by (e.g. 'Category' or 'Region') into the Rows area.
Drag the numeric column you want to measure (e.g. 'Revenue' or 'Units Sold') into the Values area. Excel defaults to Sum.
Click anywhere inside the pivot table, then go to Insert > PivotChart. Choose your chart type (bar or column is typical) and click OK.
Format the chart title and axis labels as needed, then copy or export the chart for sharing.

sheetsSteps.heading: How to Make a Pivot Chart in Google Sheets from a CSV
steps: Open Google Sheets. Go to File > Import and upload your CSV. Choose 'Replace spreadsheet' and click Import.
Select any cell in your data, then go to Insert > Pivot table. Choose 'New sheet' and click Create.
In the pivot table editor on the right, click Add next to Rows and select the column you want to group by.
Click Add next to Values, select the numeric column you want to summarize, and set the aggregation (SUM, COUNT, AVERAGE, etc.).
Select the pivot table data range, then go to Insert > Chart. Google Sheets will suggest a chart type based on the data.
Adjust the chart type in the Chart editor if needed, then click the three-dot menu on the chart to publish or download it.

distillSection.heading: A Faster Way: Skip the Manual Steps with Distill
distillSection.body: Building a pivot table before you can chart is the slowest part of the process. Distill removes that step entirely. Upload your CSV and Distill automatically detects categorical columns, groups the numeric data, and generates charts for each combination. You get the same grouped bar charts and summaries you would from a pivot chart in Excel, without touching a single field panel. It works on any CSV up to 1,000 rows and requires no account to try.

gifAlt: Screen recording of a CSV being uploaded to Distill, which automatically groups the data by category and generates a grouped bar chart showing totals per category.

faqs.q: Can I make a pivot chart without Excel?
faqs.a: Yes. Google Sheets supports pivot tables and pivot charts natively. You can also upload your CSV to Distill and get grouped charts generated automatically without building a pivot table at all.

faqs.q: Does my CSV need to be formatted a specific way for pivot charts to work?
faqs.a: Your CSV should have one row per record with clearly labeled column headers in the first row. Each row should represent a single observation, such as one transaction, one sale, or one event. Pivot tools group those rows by the categories you choose, so avoid pre-summarized data.
Yes. Distill's free tier supports CSV files up to 1,000 rows and includes all chart types. No account is required to try it.
